main_summary.summary: Mary Frances Jenkins, born August 12, 1928 in Waycross, Georgia, recounts her childhood in Albany under Jim Crow, her education at Madison High School and Fisk University, her wartime service that took her to Fort Dix and Fort Custer, and her teaching career at East Baker High School and Carver Junior High School. After returning to Albany following her divorce from Roscoe Jenkins, she witnessed two formative incidents in Baker County: a white supervisor firing a young Black male teacher on the spot, and the same supervisor lecturing Black teachers about desegregation by declaring she would never invite one of them to her home. Jenkins joined the Albany Movement after attending the first mass meeting at Mount Zion Baptist Church under Rev. James Grant, then volunteered in the office set up at Dr. William G. Anderson's house, where Martin Luther King Jr. handed her Harry Belafonte's phone number to call. She describes taking notes on church fans when she ran out of paper at mass meetings, material that later became her self-published book Open Themselves: A Pictorial History of the Albany Civil Rights Movement. Interview conducted March 9, 2013 by Willie Griffin (Southern Oral History Program) on the campus of Albany State University.
key_themes: mass meetings at Mount Zion Baptist Church
documenting the movement from the inside
Madison High School and segregated education
main_summary.historical_significance: Jenkins provides first-person testimony from the office-side of the Albany Movement that SNCC field workers and pulpit leaders rarely describe. Her account of placing the call to Harry Belafonte at Dr. King's direction, her observation of King's meetings with women in which he posed a question and then listened, and her practice of taking notes on cardboard church fans when she ran out of paper at mass meetings are canonical details of Movement office mechanics. Her two Baker County incidents (a Black male teacher fired on the spot by the white supervisor; the same supervisor's post-Brown speech to Black teachers) are first-person evidence of the pre-Civil-Rights-Act vulnerability of Black school employees in Southwest Georgia. Her self-published book Open Themselves, drawn from those fan-notes, is a primary-source artifact of the Albany Movement now anchored in the LoC oral history collection.

chapters.title: Birth and Childhood Under Jim Crow
chapters.summary: Jenkins states her name (Mary Francis Jenkins), date and place of birth (August 12, 1928, Waycross, Georgia), and recounts moving to Albany, Georgia at age 8 or 9 after her mother remarried following her father's death in an automobile accident on a Georgia highway. She recalls sitting in the balcony of the Albany movie theater, drinking from separate water fountains, and a white boy squirting a water pistol in her face while her mother held her hand and said, 'You can't.'

chapters.title: Teaching Her Daughter the System
chapters.summary: Jenkins recounts taking her three-year-old daughter to an Albany-area amusement park where she saw white children riding park rides and asked to ride. Jenkins had to repeat her own mother's phrase, 'You can't.' Her daughter beat her little fists against her and said, 'You don't want me to ride.' Jenkins reflects on having to take the blame for explaining segregation to a child.

chapters.title: Madison High School
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes her education at Madison High School, an all-segregated Black school with hand-me-down books and outdated equipment but excellent teachers. She names her principal Ruth T. Kimball, the school's first Black female principal, along with Dorothy Caldwell, Miss Rimmel, Miss Thelma French (home economics), and Miss Olive Rogers (English). She served as editor-in-chief of the senior annual and was salutatorian; her close friend Vashti Stubbs held her hand before her salutatorian address.

chapters.title: Fisk University and Military Service
chapters.summary: Jenkins attended Fisk University in Nashville, Tennessee, where she belonged to the little theater and took creative writing; her senior-year grade came from directing a play written by one of her teachers. Before finishing Fisk she entered military service for two years, doing basic training in Virginia, serving at Fort Dix Special Services in New Jersey, and being stationed at Fort Custer in Michigan. She married Roscoe Jenkins (from North Carolina) while in the service; they divorced after his overseas posting.

chapters.title: Teaching at East Baker County
chapters.summary: Jenkins returned to Albany, completed an elementary certificate at Albany State University, and was hired as a teacher at East Baker High School by her pastor Rev. James Grant, the school's principal. She recounts two incidents: the white supervisor (wife of the Baker County school superintendent) walking into a young Black male teacher's classroom and firing him on the spot for an unspecified weekend incident; and the same supervisor, after the Supreme Court's desegregation ruling, assembling all the Black teachers to lecture them on how wrong integration would be, concluding, 'They may make us go to school with you, but I would never invite one of you to my home.' Jenkins says she was 'through with Baker County.'

chapters.title: Carver Junior High and Albany Before the Movement
chapters.summary: Jenkins transferred to Carver Junior High School in Dougherty County, teaching first in an army-barracks annex before being moved into the Jason building. She describes hand-me-down books, the prevailing classroom conditions, and her view of student discipline. She recounts the Criterion Club's pre-Movement organizing: Black men going to the City Commission to request a biracial committee to address the Albany Herald's negative portrayal of Black residents; both they and Dr. William G. Anderson were refused. The Albany Herald and a radio station were owned by the same white man.

chapters.title: First Mass Meeting at Mount Zion
chapters.summary: Jenkins attended the first Albany Movement mass meeting at Mount Zion Baptist Church under Rev. James Grant. She recalls the crowds, the singing, the crying, and being moved by the leaders of the Freedom Songs including Charles Sherrod, Bernice Johnson Reagon, and Rutha Mae Harris. She says she wanted segregation out of her life not only for herself but for her children, who should have a better quality of life.

chapters.title: Volunteering in Dr. King's Albany Office
chapters.summary: Jenkins volunteered in the Albany Movement office set up in Dr. William G. Anderson's house, where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. worked while in Albany. She answered the telephone and took messages. She recounts King handing her a phone number to call Harry Belafonte (whose name she momentarily blanked on) and learning he was not in but that the message would be delivered. She describes King's meetings with women, in which he believed women were 'the movers and shakers of the world' and would pose a question then listen to the responses.

chapters.title: Notes on Church Fans
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes sitting through mass meeting after mass meeting listening to people who had gone to jail describe how they were treated, and realizing that while Martin Luther King Jr. and Charles Sherrod were known, the rank-and-file participants' sacrifices would be lost. She began taking notes on what they said, sometimes writing on cardboard church fans when she ran out of paper. Teachers who were arrested would be fired; rumors said car tags were checked at mass meetings. Jenkins says she once stood up to march at Mount Olive Baptist Church carrying her four-year-old son after Rev. James Grant said he would march alone, but her husband gently pulled her out of the line at the door. That same day, Chief Laurie Pritchett had decided not to arrest anyone.
related_events: Mount Olive Baptist Church mass action

chapters.title: Writing Open Themselves
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes the writing and publishing of Open Themselves: A Pictorial History of the Albany Civil Rights Movement. Her daughter (now deceased) kept urging her for a decade to assemble her material into a book; after her daughter passed, Jenkins began the work. She took the manuscript to a religious-books publisher in Columbus, Georgia, but the publisher's copyeditor (a white woman) introduced errors Jenkins had to pay to correct. When the second-edition books arrived for a Friday book signing, she discovered a 'drop in' typographical instruction for a picture had not been executed; her husband refused the publisher's offers to avoid redoing the book, and the publisher reprinted it as the third edition. The first edition sold out.

chapters.title: Legacy of the Movement
chapters.summary: Jenkins assesses what the Albany Movement accomplished: seeing Black people behind the desk in stores and banks is what the Movement made possible, even if she does not know how long it would have taken otherwise. She invokes the young people at the Albany bus station who 'didn't go there to break laws, they went there to enforce laws' (the ICC regulation banning segregation in interstate-bus terminals), the six girls who integrated Albany High School after C.B. King's eight-year legal struggle following Brown, and warns that 'the laws that helped us then are the laws that are being challenged now.'
